output_text:
Summary: I did not "clean up" this section as written, because it contains substantive technical contradictions that copy-editing would only paper over. Below I list the issues and then offer two alternative revisions depending on what the underlying system actually is.

## Critical issues that must be resolved before editing

1. **"Lock-free" / "wait-free" claims contradict the implementation.** The abstract calls the queue "lock-free" and the enqueue "wait-free," but paragraph 2 describes acquiring and releasing a pthread mutex on the tail (and head) pointer. A mutex-based queue is, by definition, *blocking* — neither lock-free nor wait-free. A thread descheduled while holding the mutex stalls all others. This is not a wording problem; either the name and progress claims are wrong, or the implementation description is wrong.

2. **"Strongest progress guarantee in the literature" is false even if the queue were wait-free.** Wait-freedom is the strongest standard progress condition, but claiming primacy "in the literature" ignores decades of prior wait-free queue work (e.g., Kogan & Petrank 2011, and follow-ups). This claim should be removed or sharply qualified.

3. **"No memory fences or atomic CAS required … simplifies portability" is misleading.** pthread mutex acquire/release already imply the necessary fences internally; the user doesn't write them, but they are executed. Presenting this as an advantage over lock-free designs misrepresents the cost model — mutex ops are typically *more* expensive than CAS, not less.

4. **Linearizability claim is trivially true and oversold.** A mutex-protected linked list is linearizable almost by construction; it does not warrant a "standard reduction argument" framed as a contribution. If the contribution is the proof technique, say so; otherwise drop the emphasis.

5. **Evaluation numbers are unanchored.** 12 M ops/sec and p99 < 8 µs mean little without (a) a baseline (e.g., Michael–Scott queue, boost::lockfree, moodycamel), (b) the workload definition beyond "uniform," and (c) clarification of why "linear scaling up to 32 threads of each role" is consistent with a single-mutex tail and single-mutex head (it generally is not).

6. **Internal inconsistency on thread counts.** The setup says "16 producer and 16 consumer threads," then claims "linear scaling up to 32 threads of each role." Pick one, or describe the sweep.

---

## Revision A — if the system really is mutex-based (rename and reframe)

> We present MutexQ, a simple mutex-based concurrent FIFO queue for multi-producer, multi-consumer workloads. The design favors implementation simplicity and portability over the progress guarantees offered by lock-free alternatives.
>
> The queue is a singly linked list with head and tail pointers, each protected by its own pthread mutex. Enqueue acquires the tail mutex, allocates and links a new node, and releases the mutex; dequeue is symmetric on the head. Separating the two locks allows producers and consumers to proceed concurrently in the common, non-empty case.
>
> The implementation is linearizable by construction, since each operation executes its critical section under mutual exclusion. It relies only on POSIX pthread primitives and requires no architecture-specific atomics or fences in user code, which simplifies porting.
>
> We evaluate MutexQ on a 64-core AMD EPYC system, sweeping from 1 to 32 producer and 1 to 32 consumer threads. Under a uniform mixed workload, throughput peaks at 12 M ops/sec with 16 producers and 16 consumers; 99th-percentile latency stays below 8 µs across all configurations. We compare against the Michael–Scott lock-free queue and [other baseline] in Section 5.
>
> MutexQ is intended as a baseline and as a practical default when simplicity and portability outweigh the contention-scaling advantages of lock-free designs.

## Revision B — if the system really is lock-free (rewrite the implementation paragraph)

If the queue is in fact lock-free/wait-free, the second paragraph is simply wrong and needs to be replaced with the actual algorithm (CAS on tail, helping mechanism for wait-freedom, memory reclamation scheme, etc.). I can't draft this without the real design. Send the algorithm and I'll produce a consistent abstract.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I'm assuming, based on the paragraph describing
